heroText: Helsingborg is one of Skane's key Oresund cities, facing Helsingor across the narrowest part of the strait.  The city is shaped by medieval fortifications, ferry traffic, port industry, Danish-Swedish border history, coastal parks and a compact centre around Karnan, Norra Hamnen and the waterfront.

history.text: It has been the site of permanent settlement officially since 21 May 1085.
From 1429 Eric of Pomerania introduced the Sound Dues, a levy on all trading vessels passing through the sound between Helsingør and Helsingborg.
King Charles X Gustav of Sweden landed here on 5 March 1658 to take personal possession of the Scanian lands and was met by a delegation led by the bishop of the Diocese of.
The last Danish attempt to regain Scania was in 1710, when 14,000 men landed on the shores near Helsingborg.
It took a long time to recover; even in 1770 the city had only 1,321 inhabitants and was still growing slowly.

A tramway network was inaugurated in 1903 and closed down in 1967.
Following the Swedish orthography reform of 1906, the spelling of many place names in Sweden was modernized.
In 1912, it was decided to use the form Hälsingborg.
Adolf Hitler had ordered that all Danish Jews were to be arrested and deported to the concentration camps on Rosh HaShanah, the Jewish New Year which fell on 2 October 1943.

In preparation for the local government reform in 1971, Hälsingborg city council proposed that the new, enlarged municipality should be spelled Helsingborg; this form was adopted by the government of Sweden from 1 January.

history.label: Modern city
history.text: Helsingborg's geographical position at the narrowest part of Øresund made it very important for Denmark, at that time controlling both sides of that strait.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: In World War II, Helsingborg was among the most important drop-off points for the rescue of Denmark's Jewish population during the Holocaust.
